title: I wrote an API client for my water-cooled bed
content_html:
Posted on July 18, 2026

Table of Contents:

  • Sleep
  • Chilipad
    • Why this device
    • Construction
  • API
    • A note about the Postman examples
    • Endpoints
    • Device control
  • API Client
  • Sleeping on it

Now that I have your attention, let's break that down a little bit.

I recently purchased a Sleep.me Chilipad 2.0, which is a system that allows you to control the temperature of your bed using a thin, water-filled mattress topper that's connected to a device that contains a pump, reservoir, and thermostat. It was $17001. Yes it's ridiculous. I know. I make software engineer money, I'm single, I live alone, and I don't have kids. Sue me. I'll provide a serious, grown-up justification in just a second, but for now "la la la can't hear you."

The idea is that it helps regulate your body temperature while you sleep if you have trouble doing it yourself, don't have AC in your bedroom, etc. The device allows you to set desired temperatures throughout the night, put this on schedules that apply on certain days of the week, etc. The manufacturer also provides a developer API, so immediately there was no world in which I didn't do some bullshittery with this.

The result is a tool called bedctl2. I named it that way for two reasons:

  • There's a long history of "control" programs having names that end with -ctl.
  • In my head bedctl is pronounced "bed cuttle"3, which sounds like "bed cuddle", which is cute.

Sleep

Our understanding of how the human body works is constantly evolving. This is good. However, between grifters on social media, biased research funded by industry, and the evolving understanding provided by science I mentioned earlier, you could sometimes reasonably convince yourself that there's evidence to support the claim that juggling live salmon cures male pattern baldness.4

I have a PhD in physics. I built lasers that spit out 80 femtosecond pulses, and I did quantum mechanics simulations. I am so scarred by that experience that my default stance on science-related (which includes health) headlines and social media posts is "bullshit until proven otherwise", where "proven" includes but is not limited to "corroborated by my doctor or a consensus of medical professionals." And to be clear, that distrust is not distrust of science, it's distrust that nuance has been stripped away to fit into a headline, essentially. I am not a doctor, and I'm very aware of that.

One piece of knowledge I feel safe clinging to and saying out loud to the internet is that sleep is probably good for you. Everything seems to get better with sleep: your immune system, your cognitive function, recovery from physical activity, etc.

Unfortunately, I've always had a complicated relationship with sleep. Namely because my neuro is divergent.

Neurodivergent people tend to be night owls as a survival/recovery mechanism. Having some time to ourselves where no one is placing demands on our attention, behavior, etc, gives us some space to exist as our actual selves, not our "capitalism demands that I perform on this schedule, in this manner, to these people, otherwise I can't feed myself" selves. This means that our sleep routines can be either misaligned with our work schedules, or just irregular in general.

If it's not clear yet, I have ADHD. This means that my brain never shuts up. Like, ever. This is not conducive to "winding down for bed." I developed a reading in bed routine from a pretty early age, which cuts down on distractions and acts as a signpost my nervous system can recognize as "ah, we typically fall asleep after doing this for a while, let's start that process."

Luckily I was diagnosed with ADHD pretty young, and I've been medicated since childhood. For people that are diagnosed as adults, I see one of two reactions to starting ADHD medication:

  • Holy shit, is this how normal people feel all the time? It's so much quieter!
  • Ah fuck, I'm autistic too.

If it's not clear yet, I'm autistic. The overlap between autism and ADHD is pretty significant, and in many cases the ADHD is just masking the autism. That's why some people go undiagnosed until they start medication. Not me though, I happen to be exceptionally good at burning mental energy in exchange for social skills. I was only diagnosed about two years ago, but there were signs.5

Many people with autism have sensory issues, as in certain textures or sensations are abnormally bothersome. Imagine the reaction that some people have to nails on a chalkboard, or the sound of someone chewing, etc, but getting that reaction to a texture, certain kinds of touch, etc. Similarly, many people with autism have trouble regulating their body temperature to the same degree as neurotypical people. When it comes to sleep, this means that I have trouble feeling comfortable unless the temperature is just right, the weight of the comforter/duvet is high enough, etc.

I also just moved to Portland (the cool one, not the lobster one). This is one of the rare cities in the US where air conditioning isn't a given. For most of the US this is like living in the stone age. I guess for a long time the climate was temperate enough that it wasn't needed, but the climate is changing has changed. It now gets certifiably hot as fuck in Portland.

My new apartment at least has a window unit, but it's in the living room, not the bedroom. There's also no ceiling fans. I also live in a high-rise, and heat rises. This means that my bedroom gets pretty stuffy at night.6 I bought temperature sensors to prove that my bedroom is the hottest room in the apartment and everything.

So, to recap:

  • Brain never shuts up
  • I have medical grade stimulants coursing through my veins
  • Me no likey stuffy bedroom
  • Not sleeping well is pretty bad for you
  • Please, PLEASE help me sleep better

That's why I said fuck it and dropped RAM money on a temperature control system for my bed. Spoiler: it's good.

Chilipad

Now we'll talk about the device itself.

Why this device

Naturally I did a bunch of research into the "sleep cooler" landscape before buying this.

I didn't want a fan-based solution like BedJet because I didn't want it blowing cold air at my feet in the summer, even if blowing warm air on my feet during the winter sounds nice.

I think the closest competitor is Eight Sleep, but I dismissed it pretty much immediately. The cheapest model is $3000, it requires a subscription, there's some AI bullshit built-in, etc. PASS.7 Seriously, fuck off with a subscription in order to keep using the $3000 device you just bought. Do not buy that device.

The Chilipad 2.0, which I'll just call the Chilipad going forward out of laziness, starts cheaper because it's modular. You can buy a unit for just one side of the bed to save some money, which is what I did. This also means that if you buy two of them you can set them to different temperatures. Every partner I've ever lived with has been a human furnace while sleeping, so I can see this being helpful for couples.

There's a bunch of other very polite things that the company has done that sealed the deal for me:

  • Even though it's WiFi connected, there's an airplane mode.
  • You can control the device with an included Bluetooth remote, but I haven't checked or tested whether airplane mode is actual airplane mode or just "disable WiFi."
  • They provide a developer API.8
  • They provide a Postman "collection" for playing around with the API.
  • They provide an online Swagger UI and OpenAPI schema.

The ability to put the device into airplane mode tells me that they're conscious of the fact that it can be creepy to have an internet-connected device built into your bed, and that you may want a failsafe if your bed joins a robocult botnet.

Construction

The device consists of two main parts:

  • The "dock", which is the control unit, thermostat, pump, etc.
  • The cover, which contains the "hydrolayer".

I'll let the photos on the website speak for themselves for the most part. The dock sits under the foot of my bed, which is close to the door to my bedroom so the heat exchanger isn't just spewing hot air back into my room.

The cover itself is actually three layers:

  • The bottom layer
  • The hydrolayer (a TPU membrane that water circulates through)
  • The top cover

The top cover zips open so you can install the hydrolayer on the side of the bed that you sleep on. The top cover is made of some fabric that's cool to the touch and is lightly padded so you don't feel like you're laying directly on the hydrolayer.

API

Let's talk about the API so we can talk about the API client. It looks like the documentation is gated by having an account, so I'll describe it for you. First you need to generate a token for yourself, like with most APIs.

A note about the Postman examples

I'm not going to talk about these. They're helpful when the documentation is lacking detail (which is rare), but I wish this wasn't tied to Postman. I think it's overkill, and a bit cluttered. I also don't love that you need an account with some other company. I'd prefer something open source, like hurl scripts or whatever. Bah humbug.

There's also typos in a variable name in all examples except for one: {{SleepMeAPI_URL}} should be {{SleepMeAPI_Url}} (notice the casing of Url).

On the upside, the provided body of the "Set Device Status" example is

{
    "set_temperature_f": 69,
    "thermal_control_status": "standby"
}

Nice9

Endpoints

The API is pretty simple, you get two endpoints:

  • /devices
    • GET: list the devices associated with the token
  • /devices/{:id}
    • GET: gets all information about the device
    • PATCH: set device properties

The PATCH request is how you control the device remotely. We'll discuss that in a second.

Here's what a call to the /devices endpoint gets you (I'll be redacting some things10):

[
  {
    "id": "<redacted>",
    "name": "Dock",
    "attachments": []
  }
]

This is a list of devices, where each object contains the device ID, user-assigned name, and any other devices attached to it (I think, not sure about the last one).

Here's what you get back from /devices/{:id}:

{
  "about": {
    "firmware_version": "1.12.1358",
    "ip_address": "<redacted>",
    "lan_address": "192.168.8.69",
    "mac_address": "<redacted>",
    "model": "DP723NA",
    "serial_number": "<redacted>"
  },
  "control": {
    "brightness_level": 100,
    "display_temperature_unit": "f",
    "set_temperature_c": 21.5,
    "set_temperature_f": 71,
    "thermal_control_status": "standby",
    "time_zone": "America/Los_Angeles"
  },
  "status": {
    "is_connected": true,
    "is_water_low": false,
    "water_level": 100,
    "water_temperature_f": -1,
    "water_temperature_c": -1
  }
}

The about object contains read-only data about the device identity and definition. ip_address is the external IP and lan_address is the IP on your local network.

The control object describes the current settings of all controllable properties. This is device specific, so some devices may have a subset of these, or may have keys not listed in the documentation (the documentation doesn't list the brightness_level key, for example). thermal_control_status is either standby or active, and is just whether the device is actively controlling the temperature.

The status object contains read-only data about the status of the device. is_connected is whether the device is reachable from the API. water_level is a percentage (0-100).

Temperatures are a little funky. The Fahrenheit temperatures seem to be integers whereas the Celsius temperatures are floats (Javascript "all numbers are floats" aside). The allowed Fahrenheit range is 55-115, and the Celsius range is 13.0-48.0. In control.set_temperature_* you can set the temperature to -1/-1.0 for MAX_COLD, or 999/999.0 for MAX_HEAT. In status.water_temperature_* presumably -1 is a sentinel value while the device is in standby. That's not documented, but seems reasonable.

Aside: Fahrenheit vs. Celsius

To my dying breath I will fight Canadians and Europeans that Celsius is the better temperature unit for human-facing temperatures. Example: air temperature. 0F is really cold (0% hot) and 100F is really hot (100% hot). 0C is cold but not outrageous, but 100C is "100% dead." Also, as an AC connosieur, sometimes one degree F really does take you from "almost there" to "perfect." Throw off your chains. Be free of the coarse grained tyranny of Celsius.

Device control

You set the properties of the device by sending a control object with just the keys that you want to set. For example, if you only want to set the water temperature, you'd send

{
  "set_temperature_f": 73
}

This isn't actually spelled out in the documentation, but I've gathered it from the Postman collection. The important bit is that you don't need to include all of the keys, and you don't need to wrap it in a { "control": ... }.

API Client

I wrote bedctl in Zig, because that's my language of choice these days. I've used Rust professionally for about 5 years, and as a hobbyist since 2018. No shade, I just want instant feedback and low mental overhead. zig build check --watch -fincremental updates in 75ms on my M1 Max Macbook Pro, which means that it's not even using the faster self-hosted x86_64 backend. The struggles I have are struggles of learning a new tool, not "how do I even read this" or "how do I even track down this trait method implementation."

Anyway.

Here's some examples of what it looks like to use:

$ bedctl devices
Dock: id=<redacted>

$ bedctl status
About:
  Firmware version: 1.12.1358
  External IPv4: <redacted>
  Internal IPv4: 192.168.8.121
  MAC Address: <redacted>
  Model: DP723NA
  Serial Number: <redacted>
Settings:
  Brightness: 100/100
  Temperature Unit: F
  Temperature: 78F / 25.5C
  Thermal Controls: Off
  Time Zone: America/Los_Angeles
Status:
  Connected: Yes
  Water Low: No
  Water Level: 100/100
  Observed Temperature: 72F / 22.0C

$ bedctl set temp 74

In those examples I've set the token and device ID with environment variables, but you can also pass them with --token and --device flags.

bedctl is mainly a library, and the CLI is a very thin wrapper around that library. The current structure of the project looks like this:

  • bedctl Zig module
  • bedctl CLI that consumes the Zig module

My ultimate goal is for bedctl to be consumed as a zero-dependency dynamic library from higher level languages. I would love for someone to write a Python wrapper for use in Home Assistant. I'm not going to do that, but I would use it if someone did. I don't have the build or interface set up for the dynamic library yet, but that will come at some point in the near future. At that point I'll have a separate build step for a CLI that consumes it as a dynamic library for dogfooding the C interface.

At that point the project structure will look like this:

  • bedctl Zig module
  • bedctl CLI that consumes the Zig module
  • libbedctl dynamic library
    • Provides a C interface to the Zig module
  • bedctl-dl CLI that consumes the dynamic library

The CLI only has one dependency: an argument parser I extracted from the Tigerbeetle codebase, tweaked, and updated for compatibility with Zig 0.16.0. The library has no dependencies outside of the Zig standard library, using std.http.Client for requests. The CLI also doesn't link against libc, which is some magic made possible by Zig. Once the dynamic library is set up, it won't link against libc either. Also, since Zig natively supports cross-compilation, I can build for any supported system without needing access to it. Love it.

These are the central data structures that the library operates on:

pub const Device = struct {
    /// The device's unique identifier.
    id: []const u8,
    /// The user-provided name for the device
    name: []const u8,
    /// The devices attached to this one, I think.
    attachments: []const []const u8,

    /// Read-only data about the identity and definition of the device.
    pub const About = struct {
        /// The current loaded firmware of the device.
        firmware_version: []const u8,
        /// The public IPv4 address of the WiFi the device is connected to.
        ip_address: []const u8,
        /// The LAN IP given to the device by the router.
        lan_address: []const u8,
        /// The MAC address of the control board inside the device.
        mac_address: []const u8,
        /// The model of the device.
        model: []const u8,
        /// The serial number of the device.
        serial_number: []const u8,
    };

    /// The current state of the device's controllable properties,
    /// indicating that you can request to change them.
    ///
    /// Availability may vary depending on device model.
    pub const Control = struct {
        /// The brightness level of the device's display
        /// as a percentage (range: 0-100).
        brightness_level: ?u8,
        /// How the temperature is shown on the device's display.
        display_temperature_unit: ?enum { c, f },
        /// The desired water temperature setting in Celsius.
        ///
        /// The allowed values are as follows:
        /// - MAX_COLD is -1.0
        /// - MAX_HEAT is 999.0
        /// - Normal temperature range is 13.0-48.0 inclusive
        ///
        /// The value will not be validated before sending the request.
        set_temperature_c: ?f32,
        /// The desired water temperature setting in Fahrenheit.
        ///
        /// The temperature may only be set between 55-115 inclusive.
        /// This will not be validated before sending the request.
        set_temperature_f: ?enum {
          max_cold = -1,
          max_heat = 999,
          _,
        },
        /// The state of the system's thermal control systems.
        ///
        /// Changing to "active" will turn the systems on, and
        /// a change to "standby" will turn them off.
        thermal_control_status: ?enum { active, standby },
        /// The time zone set on the device.
        ///
        /// May be set to any IANA time zone name.
        time_zone: ?[]const u8,
    };

    /// Read-only data about the status of the device.
    pub const Status = struct {
        /// Indicates if the device is actively connected and reachable.
        is_connected: bool,
        /// Indicates if the water level is low enough that it needs to be refilled.
        is_water_low: bool,
        /// Indicates the fill level of the resevoir as a percentage (range: 0-100).
        water_level: u8,
        /// Indicates the water temperature in degrees Fahrenheit (range: 55-115).
        ///
        /// If the device is in standby, this will be -1.
        water_temperature_f: i8,
        /// Indicates the water temperature in degrees Celsius (range: 13.0-48.0).
        ///
        /// If the device is in standby, this will be -1.
        water_temperature_c: f32,
    };
};

/// A device status response.
pub const DeviceStatus = struct {
    about: Device.About,
    control: Device.Control,
    status: Device.Status,
};

These are largely translations of the JSON objects, but we get to be fancy in a couple of cases. Rather than strings for control.display_temperature_unit we get an anonymous enum with .c and .f variants, same thing for control.thermal_control_status with .active and .standby variants. Anonymous structs and enums are one of my favorite syntax features in Zig. Being able to avoid naming something that's clear from context cuts down on visual noise and the mental overhead of coming up with a concise, unambiguous name.11

We also get a little bit of extra type-system assistance when setting temperatures in Fahrenheit. I forget the name of this particular feature, but when defining an enum you can give a semantic name to specific integer values, and let the rest just pass through as their literal integer values. This allows you to set both .max_cold and 78 as literal values, rather than needing to remember that -1 is the magic value for MAX_COLD. Unfortunately there's no way (that I'm aware of) to encode the additional restriction that the valid range of temperatures is 55-115F. For Celsius we're pretty much in YOLO mode because it's a float.

The experience of writing it was pretty pleasant. It was my first time using the Io interface for HTTP operations, so there was a little bit of floundering at first.

I think most people's mental model of sending a request is something like:

  • Provide a URI
  • Provide a body either as bytes or a string
  • send
  • Get either bytes or a string back

Since Zig is lower level, and the HTTP client in the standard library isn't intended to be the Easy Button of HTTP clients, things look pretty different. The process looks like this:

  • Create a URI object
  • Mutate the URI object to set the path
  • Create a request object with the base URI, headers, and connection settings
  • (In this case) Send the request head
  • Receive the response head
  • This allows you to get a reader for the body
  • Consume from the reader to get the body

It does feel a little bit like using scalpel-level precision for all operations, but that's okay. I do like how with Rust you get to be high level in many places, and then drop down to low-level, nitty-gritty sweat mode when you feel like it. In Zig it feels like I'm always in sweat mode.

The methods for sending the GET request to the /devices endpoint look like this:

/// List the devices registered to the provided token.
pub fn devices(self: *Self) Error![]const Device {
    var request = try self.requestGetPath(DEVICES);
    defer request.deinit();
    const response = try self.fetch(&request);
    const parsed = try std.json.parseFromSliceLeaky([]const Device, self.gpa, response, .{
        .allocate = .alloc_always,
    });
    return parsed;
}

/// Constructs a GET request configured with the authorization header.
///
/// Caller must deinit the request.
fn requestGetPath(self: *Self, path: []const u8) Error!Client.Request {
    var endpoint = self.uri;
    endpoint.path = .{ .percent_encoded = path };
    const request = try self.client.request(http.Method.GET, endpoint, .{
        .keep_alive = true,
        .headers = .{
            .authorization = .{ .override = self.token },
        },
    });
    return request;
}

/// Sends the request and reads the response. Caller is response for
/// freeing the returned slice.
fn fetch(self: *Self, request: *Client.Request) Error![]const u8 {
    try request.sendBodiless();
    var redirect_buffer: [1024]u8 = undefined;
    var response = try request.receiveHead(&redirect_buffer);
    var response_buffer: [4096]u8 = undefined;
    const response_body = try response.reader(&response_buffer).allocRemaining(self.gpa, .limited(64_000));
    return response_body;
}

Ignore the Leaky in parseFromSliceLeaky, I'm doing some stuff with arena allocators not shown here that makes this a non-issue. Trust me bro.

Sleeping on it

So, what's it like sleeping on my $1700 gadget?

I've slept really well for the last couple of nights. I wear a fitness tracker, so I can also see quantitatively that my sleep is much better. I've spent more time in the "restorative sleep" phases, and I've had fewer "wake events" over the course of the night. Subjectively, I also feel pretty well rested. Another data point is that one of the nights I've slept on it was after having some whiskey, which I've learned from my fitness tracker typically makes my sleep much worse, but I actually slept pretty well that night.12

I've only had it a few days (I wrote bedctl over the course of about 3 nights), so we're still squarely in the honeymoon phase.

The first time I got into the bed with the temperature set a bit too low it almost felt like I was laying in a wet spot because of the cooling sensation. Adjusting the temperature helps, and I don't feel like I'm laying in a wet spot anymore. I'll also say that the temperature adjusts pretty quickly. I haven't timed it, but I'd put it closer to 1 minute than I would 10 minutes. The device took less than 1 gallon of water to fill, so there's not a ton of thermal mass to heat/cool.

I'm still dialing in my preferred, scheduled temperatures, but the included remote (which is basically just a big dial with a touch screen) makes it really easy to adjust the temperature on the fly. The dial on the remote is not the most satisfying tactile experience, but at least it vibrates at temperature increments so you don't have to look at it to know how much you're adjusting it. The haptic feedback is helpful for making adjustments if you have to wear a sleep mask because your bedroom has gorgeous floor to ceiling windows that are too tall to reasonably install blackout curtains on, and if there's a big frosted glass window built into your bedroom door that lets in light from the living room.13

Because my brain is different, I still need a slight amount of airflow from a fan to feel comfy. Previously I would have to balance these things:

  • Enough airflow to keep the room cool
  • Direct airflow on me because brain needs that sensation
  • Not so much air directly on me that it dries out my airways and makes me cough all day

Now I just have the fan on the lowest setting.

Setting the temperature slightly warm in the morning feels pretty cozy, and I'm really looking forward to it pre-warming my bed in the winter. That said, I can tell that the breathability of the "thing I'm laying on top of, mattress or otherwise" is reduced. To be clear, it doesn't feel like I'm laying on plastic or anything like that, just that the skin in contact with the bed was like 1% sweaty with the temperature cranked a little bit. Also keep in mind that my body is apparently a weak bitch that can't control its own temperature very well, so you may be blissfully dry in the same scenario.

On that note, that's about all I have to say. Positive experiences so far. I'll probably post an update when I get around to setting up the dynamic library. Night night.

1

Some might say that it's eye wateringly expensive lolololol.

2

It's pronounced "bedctl".

3

Ok, I lied.

4

My hairline is doing great, thanks for asking.

5

I'll take my water with exactly 6 ice cubes, please. And make sure the glass doesn't smell bad. And make sure the rim isn't too thick.

6

Pre-Chilipad solution: put a tower fan outside the bedroom and have it blow lukewarm air into the stuffy bedroom.

7

There's apparently a jailbreaking community out there, which is awesome, but I didn't want to do that out of the box.

8

Did I misread and think that the API was local instead of talking to their cloud service? Yes. ADHD in action, folks.

9

If I just blew up an engineer's spot, sorry. Sleep.me if you're reading this be cool.

10

Please don't ruin my life hackerman.

11

I'm anal about names. I held the title of High Kind of Bikeshedding at my last company. I'm not making that up.

12

Don't misinterpret this. Alcohol is still bad for you, and bad for your sleep. At best this is probably just helping you sleep less bad.

13

A+ design.

Ah fuck, I'm autistic too. If it's not clear yet, I'm autistic. The overlap between autism and ADHD is pretty significant, and in many cases the ADHD is just masking the autism. That's why some people go undiagnosed until they start medication. Not me though, I happen to be exceptionally good at burning mental energy in exchange for social skills. I was only diagnosed about two years ago, but there were signs.5 Many people with autism have sensory issues, as in certain textures or sensations are abnormally bothersome. Imagine the reaction that some people have to nails on a chalkboard, or the sound of someone chewing, etc, but getting that reaction to a texture, certain kinds of touch, etc. Similarly, many people with autism have trouble regulating their body temperature to the same degree as neurotypical people. When it comes to sleep, this means that I have trouble feeling comfortable unless the temperature is just right, the weight of the comforter/duvet is high enough, etc. I also just moved to Portland (the cool one, not the lobster one). This is one of the rare cities in the US where air conditioning isn't a given. For most of the US this is like living in the stone age. I guess for a long time the climate was temperate enough that it wasn't needed, but the climate is changing has changed. It now gets certifiably hot as fuck in Portland. My new apartment at least has a window unit, but it's in the living room, not the bedroom. There's also no ceiling fans. I also live in a high-rise, and heat rises. This means that my bedroom gets pretty stuffy at night.6 I bought temperature sensors to prove that my bedroom is the hottest room in the apartment and everything. So, to recap: Brain never shuts up I have medical grade stimulants coursing through my veins Me no likey stuffy bedroom Not sleeping well is pretty bad for you Please, PLEASE help me sleep better That's why I said fuck it and dropped RAM money on a temperature control system for my bed. Spoiler: it's good. Chilipad Now we'll talk about the device itself. Why this device Naturally I did a bunch of research into the "sleep cooler" landscape before buying this. I didn't want a fan-based solution like BedJet because I didn't want it blowing cold air at my feet in the summer, even if blowing warm air on my feet during the winter sounds nice. I think the closest competitor is Eight Sleep, but I dismissed it pretty much immediately. The cheapest model is $3000, it requires a subscription, there's some AI bullshit built-in, etc. PASS.7 Seriously, fuck off with a subscription in order to keep using the $3000 device you just bought. Do not buy that device. The Chilipad 2.0, which I'll just call the Chilipad going forward out of laziness, starts cheaper because it's modular. You can buy a unit for just one side of the bed to save some money, which is what I did. This also means that if you buy two of them you can set them to different temperatures. Every partner I've ever lived with has been a human furnace while sleeping, so I can see this being helpful for couples. There's a bunch of other very polite things that the company has done that sealed the deal for me: Even though it's WiFi connected, there's an airplane mode. You can control the device with an included Bluetooth remote, but I haven't checked or tested whether airplane mode is actual airplane mode or just "disable WiFi." They provide a developer API.8 They provide a Postman "collection" for playing around with the API. They provide an online Swagger UI and OpenAPI schema. The ability to put the device into airplane mode tells me that they're conscious of the fact that it can be creepy to have an internet-connected device built into your bed, and that you may want a failsafe if your bed joins a robocult botnet. Construction The device consists of two main parts: The "dock", which is the control unit, thermostat, pump, etc. The cover, which contains the "hydrolayer". I'll let the photos on the website speak for themselves for the most part. The dock sits under the foot of my bed, which is close to the door to my bedroom so the heat exchanger isn't just spewing hot air back into my room. The cover itself is actually three layers: The bottom layer The hydrolayer (a TPU membrane that water circulates through) The top cover The top cover zips open so you can install the hydrolayer on the side of the bed that you sleep on. The top cover is made of some fabric that's cool to the touch and is lightly padded so you don't feel like you're laying directly on the hydrolayer. API Let's talk about the API so we can talk about the API client. It looks like the documentation is gated by having an account, so I'll describe it for you. First you need to generate a token for yourself, like with most APIs. A note about the Postman examples I'm not going to talk about these. They're helpful when the documentation is lacking detail (which is rare), but I wish this wasn't tied to Postman. I think it's overkill, and a bit cluttered. I also don't love that you need an account with some other company. I'd prefer something open source, like hurl scripts or whatever. Bah humbug. There's also typos in a variable name in all examples except for one: {{SleepMeAPI_URL}} should be {{SleepMeAPI_Url}} (notice the casing of Url). On the upside, the provided body of the "Set Device Status" example is { "set_temperature_f": 69, "thermal_control_status": "standby" } Nice9 Endpoints The API is pretty simple, you get two endpoints: /devices GET: list the devices associated with the token /devices/{:id} GET: gets all information about the device PATCH: set device properties The PATCH request is how you control the device remotely. We'll discuss that in a second. Here's what a call to the /devices endpoint gets you (I'll be redacting some things10): [ { "id": "<redacted>", "name": "Dock", "attachments": [] } ] This is a list of devices, where each object contains the device ID, user-assigned name, and any other devices attached to it (I think, not sure about the last one). Here's what you get back from /devices/{:id}: { "about": { "firmware_version": "1.12.1358", "ip_address": "<redacted>", "lan_address": "192.168.8.69", "mac_address": "<redacted>", "model": "DP723NA", "serial_number": "<redacted>" }, "control": { "brightness_level": 100, "display_temperature_unit": "f", "set_temperature_c": 21.5, "set_temperature_f": 71, "thermal_control_status": "standby", "time_zone": "America/Los_Angeles" }, "status": { "is_connected": true, "is_water_low": false, "water_level": 100, "water_temperature_f": -1, "water_temperature_c": -1 } } The about object contains read-only data about the device identity and definition. ip_address is the external IP and lan_address is the IP on your local network. The control object describes the current settings of all controllable properties. This is device specific, so some devices may have a subset of these, or may have keys not listed in the documentation (the documentation doesn't list the brightness_level key, for example). thermal_control_status is either standby or active, and is just whether the device is actively controlling the temperature. The status object contains read-only data about the status of the device. is_connected is whether the device is reachable from the API. water_level is a percentage (0-100). Temperatures are a little funky. The Fahrenheit temperatures seem to be integers whereas the Celsius temperatures are floats (Javascript "all numbers are floats" aside). The allowed Fahrenheit range is 55-115, and the Celsius range is 13.0-48.0. In control.set_temperature_* you can set the temperature to -1/-1.0 for MAX_COLD, or 999/999.0 for MAX_HEAT. In status.water_temperature_* presumably -1 is a sentinel value while the device is in standby. That's not documented, but seems reasonable. Aside: Fahrenheit vs. Celsius To my dying breath I will fight Canadians and Europeans that Celsius is the better temperature unit for human-facing temperatures. Example: air temperature. 0F is really cold (0% hot) and 100F is really hot (100% hot). 0C is cold but not outrageous, but 100C is "100% dead." Also, as an AC connosieur, sometimes one degree F really does take you from "almost there" to "perfect." Throw off your chains. Be free of the coarse grained tyranny of Celsius. Device control You set the properties of the device by sending a control object with just the keys that you want to set. For example, if you only want to set the water temperature, you'd send { "set_temperature_f": 73 } This isn't actually spelled out in the documentation, but I've gathered it from the Postman collection. The important bit is that you don't need to include all of the keys, and you don't need to wrap it in a { "control": ... }. API Client I wrote bedctl in Zig, because that's my language of choice these days. I've used Rust professionally for about 5 years, and as a hobbyist since 2018. No shade, I just want instant feedback and low mental overhead. zig build check --watch -fincremental updates in 75ms on my M1 Max Macbook Pro, which means that it's not even using the faster self-hosted x86_64 backend. The struggles I have are struggles of learning a new tool, not "how do I even read this" or "how do I even track down this trait method implementation." Anyway. Here's some examples of what it looks like to use: $ bedctl devices Dock: id=<redacted> $ bedctl status About: Firmware version: 1.12.1358 External IPv4: <redacted> Internal IPv4: 192.168.8.121 MAC Address: <redacted> Model: DP723NA Serial Number: <redacted> Settings: Brightness: 100/100 Temperature Unit: F Temperature: 78F / 25.5C Thermal Controls: Off Time Zone: America/Los_Angeles Status: Connected: Yes Water Low: No Water Level: 100/100 Observed Temperature: 72F / 22.0C $ bedctl set temp 74 In those examples I've set the token and device ID with environment variables, but you can also pass them with --token and --device flags. bedctl is mainly a library, and the CLI is a very thin wrapper around that library. The current structure of the project looks like this: bedctl Zig module bedctl CLI that consumes the Zig module My ultimate goal is for bedctl to be consumed as a zero-dependency dynamic library from higher level languages. I would love for someone to write a Python wrapper for use in Home Assistant. I'm not going to do that, but I would use it if someone did. I don't have the build or interface set up for the dynamic library yet, but that will come at some point in the near future. At that point I'll have a separate build step for a CLI that consumes it as a dynamic library for dogfooding the C interface. At that point the project structure will look like this: bedctl Zig module bedctl CLI that consumes the Zig module libbedctl dynamic library Provides a C interface to the Zig module bedctl-dl CLI that consumes the dynamic library The CLI only has one dependency: an argument parser I extracted from the Tigerbeetle codebase, tweaked, and updated for compatibility with Zig 0.16.0. The library has no dependencies outside of the Zig standard library, using std.http.Client for requests. The CLI also doesn't link against libc, which is some magic made possible by Zig. Once the dynamic library is set up, it won't link against libc either. Also, since Zig natively supports cross-compilation, I can build for any supported system without needing access to it. Love it. These are the central data structures that the library operates on: pub const Device = struct { /// The device's unique identifier. id: []const u8, /// The user-provided name for the device name: []const u8, /// The devices attached to this one, I think. attachments: []const []const u8, /// Read-only data about the identity and definition of the device. pub const About = struct { /// The current loaded firmware of the device. firmware_version: []const u8, /// The public IPv4 address of the WiFi the device is connected to. ip_address: []const u8, /// The LAN IP given to the device by the router. lan_address: []const u8, /// The MAC address of the control board inside the device. mac_address: []const u8, /// The model of the device. model: []const u8, /// The serial number of the device. serial_number: []const u8, }; /// The current state of the device's controllable properties, /// indicating that you can request to change them. /// /// Availability may vary depending on device model. pub const Control = struct { /// The brightness level of the device's display /// as a percentage (range: 0-100). brightness_level: ?u8, /// How the temperature is shown on the device's display. display_temperature_unit: ?enum { c, f }, /// The desired water temperature setting in Celsius. /// /// The allowed values are as follows: /// - MAX_COLD is -1.0 /// - MAX_HEAT is 999.0 /// - Normal temperature range is 13.0-48.0 inclusive /// /// The value will not be validated before sending the request. set_temperature_c: ?f32, /// The desired water temperature setting in Fahrenheit. /// /// The temperature may only be set between 55-115 inclusive. /// This will not be validated before sending the request. set_temperature_f: ?enum { max_cold = -1, max_heat = 999, _, }, /// The state of the system's thermal control systems. /// /// Changing to "active" will turn the systems on, and /// a change to "standby" will turn them off. thermal_control_status: ?enum { active, standby }, /// The time zone set on the device. /// /// May be set to any IANA time zone name. time_zone: ?[]const u8, }; /// Read-only data about the status of the device. pub const Status = struct { /// Indicates if the device is actively connected and reachable. is_connected: bool, /// Indicates if the water level is low enough that it needs to be refilled. is_water_low: bool, /// Indicates the fill level of the resevoir as a percentage (range: 0-100). water_level: u8, /// Indicates the water temperature in degrees Fahrenheit (range: 55-115). /// /// If the device is in standby, this will be -1. water_temperature_f: i8, /// Indicates the water temperature in degrees Celsius (range: 13.0-48.0). /// /// If the device is in standby, this will be -1. water_temperature_c: f32, }; }; /// A device status response. pub const DeviceStatus = struct { about: Device.About, control: Device.Control, status: Device.Status, }; These are largely translations of the JSON objects, but we get to be fancy in a couple of cases. Rather than strings for control.display_temperature_unit we get an anonymous enum with .c and .f variants, same thing for control.thermal_control_status with .active and .standby variants. Anonymous structs and enums are one of my favorite syntax features in Zig. Being able to avoid naming something that's clear from context cuts down on visual noise and the mental overhead of coming up with a concise, unambiguous name.11 We also get a little bit of extra type-system assistance when setting temperatures in Fahrenheit. I forget the name of this particular feature, but when defining an enum you can give a semantic name to specific integer values, and let the rest just pass through as their literal integer values. This allows you to set both .max_cold and 78 as literal values, rather than needing to remember that -1 is the magic value for MAX_COLD. Unfortunately there's no way (that I'm aware of) to encode the additional restriction that the valid range of temperatures is 55-115F. For Celsius we're pretty much in YOLO mode because it's a float. The experience of writing it was pretty pleasant. It was my first time using the Io interface for HTTP operations, so there was a little bit of floundering at first. I think most people's mental model of sending a request is something like: Provide a URI Provide a body either as bytes or a string send Get either bytes or a string back Since Zig is lower level, and the HTTP client in the standard library isn't intended to be the Easy Button of HTTP clients, things look pretty different. The process looks like this: Create a URI object Mutate the URI object to set the path Create a request object with the base URI, headers, and connection settings (In this case) Send the request head Receive the response head This allows you to get a reader for the body Consume from the reader to get the body It does feel a little bit like using scalpel-level precision for all operations, but that's okay. I do like how with Rust you get to be high level in many places, and then drop down to low-level, nitty-gritty sweat mode when you feel like it. In Zig it feels like I'm always in sweat mode. The methods for sending the GET request to the /devices endpoint look like this: /// List the devices registered to the provided token. pub fn devices(self: *Self) Error![]const Device { var request = try self.requestGetPath(DEVICES); defer request.deinit(); const response = try self.fetch(&request); const parsed = try std.json.parseFromSliceLeaky([]const Device, self.gpa, response, .{ .allocate = .alloc_always, }); return parsed; } /// Constructs a GET request configured with the authorization header. /// /// Caller must deinit the request. fn requestGetPath(self: *Self, path: []const u8) Error!Client.Request { var endpoint = self.uri; endpoint.path = .{ .percent_encoded = path }; const request = try self.client.request(http.Method.GET, endpoint, .{ .keep_alive = true, .headers = .{ .authorization = .{ .override = self.token }, }, }); return request; } /// Sends the request and reads the response. Caller is response for /// freeing the returned slice. fn fetch(self: *Self, request: *Client.Request) Error![]const u8 { try request.sendBodiless(); var redirect_buffer: [1024]u8 = undefined; var response = try request.receiveHead(&redirect_buffer); var response_buffer: [4096]u8 = undefined; const response_body = try response.reader(&response_buffer).allocRemaining(self.gpa, .limited(64_000)); return response_body; } Ignore the Leaky in parseFromSliceLeaky, I'm doing some stuff with arena allocators not shown here that makes this a non-issue. Trust me bro. Sleeping on it So, what's it like sleeping on my $1700 gadget? I've slept really well for the last couple of nights. I wear a fitness tracker, so I can also see quantitatively that my sleep is much better. I've spent more time in the "restorative sleep" phases, and I've had fewer "wake events" over the course of the night. Subjectively, I also feel pretty well rested. Another data point is that one of the nights I've slept on it was after having some whiskey, which I've learned from my fitness tracker typically makes my sleep much worse, but I actually slept pretty well that night.12 I've only had it a few days (I wrote bedctl over the course of about 3 nights), so we're still squarely in the honeymoon phase. The first time I got into the bed with the temperature set a bit too low it almost felt like I was laying in a wet spot because of the cooling sensation. Adjusting the temperature helps, and I don't feel like I'm laying in a wet spot anymore. I'll also say that the temperature adjusts pretty quickly. I haven't timed it, but I'd put it closer to 1 minute than I would 10 minutes. The device took less than 1 gallon of water to fill, so there's not a ton of thermal mass to heat/cool. I'm still dialing in my preferred, scheduled temperatures, but the included remote (which is basically just a big dial with a touch screen) makes it really easy to adjust the temperature on the fly. The dial on the remote is not the most satisfying tactile experience, but at least it vibrates at temperature increments so you don't have to look at it to know how much you're adjusting it. The haptic feedback is helpful for making adjustments if you have to wear a sleep mask because your bedroom has gorgeous floor to ceiling windows that are too tall to reasonably install blackout curtains on, and if there's a big frosted glass window built into your bedroom door that lets in light from the living room.13 Because my brain is different, I still need a slight amount of airflow from a fan to feel comfy. Previously I would have to balance these things: Enough airflow to keep the room cool Direct airflow on me because brain needs that sensation Not so much air directly on me that it dries out my airways and makes me cough all day Now I just have the fan on the lowest setting. Setting the temperature slightly warm in the morning feels pretty cozy, and I'm really looking forward to it pre-warming my bed in the winter. That said, I can tell that the breathability of the "thing I'm laying on top of, mattress or otherwise" is reduced. To be clear, it doesn't feel like I'm laying on plastic or anything like that, just that the skin in contact with the bed was like 1% sweaty with the temperature cranked a little bit. Also keep in mind that my body is apparently a weak bitch that can't control its own temperature very well, so you may be blissfully dry in the same scenario. On that note, that's about all I have to say. Positive experiences so far. I'll probably post an update when I get around to setting up the dynamic library. Night night. 1Some might say that it's eye wateringly expensive lolololol. 2It's pronounced "bedctl". 3Ok, I lied. 4My hairline is doing great, thanks for asking. 5I'll take my water with exactly 6 ice cubes, please. And make sure the glass doesn't smell bad. And make sure the rim isn't too thick. 6Pre-Chilipad solution: put a tower fan outside the bedroom and have it blow lukewarm air into the stuffy bedroom. 7There's apparently a jailbreaking community out there, which is awesome, but I didn't want to do that out of the box. 8Did I misread and think that the API was local instead of talking to their cloud service? Yes. ADHD in action, folks. 9If I just blew up an engineer's spot, sorry. Sleep.me if you're reading this be cool. 10Please don't ruin my life hackerman. 11I'm anal about names. I held the title of High Kind of Bikeshedding at my last company. I'm not making that up. 12Don't misinterpret this. Alcohol is still bad for you, and bad for your sleep. At best this is probably just helping you sleep less bad. 13A+ design.

blocks.html_en: I recently purchased a Sleep.me Chilipad 2.0, which is a system that allows you to control the temperature of your bed using a thin, water-filled mattress topper that's connected to a device that contains a pump, reservoir, and thermostat. It was $17001. Yes it's ridiculous. I know. I make software engineer money, I'm single, I live alone, and I don't have kids. Sue me. I'll provide a serious, grown-up justification in just a second, but for now "la la la can't hear you."

blocks.html_en: The idea is that it helps regulate your body temperature while you sleep if you have trouble doing it yourself, don't have AC in your bedroom, etc. The device allows you to set desired temperatures throughout the night, put this on schedules that apply on certain days of the week, etc. The manufacturer also provides a developer API, so immediately there was no world in which I didn't do some bullshittery with this.

blocks.html_en: Our understanding of how the human body works is constantly evolving. This is good. However, between grifters on social media, biased research funded by industry, and the evolving understanding provided by science I mentioned earlier, you could sometimes reasonably convince yourself that there's evidence to support the claim that juggling live salmon cures male pattern baldness.4

blocks.html_en: I have a PhD in physics. I built lasers that spit out 80 femtosecond pulses, and I did quantum mechanics simulations. I am so scarred by that experience that my default stance on science-related (which includes health) headlines and social media posts is "bullshit until proven otherwise", where "proven" includes but is not limited to "corroborated by my doctor or a consensus of medical professionals." And to be clear, that distrust is not distrust of science, it's distrust that nuance has been stripped away to fit into a headline, essentially. I am not a doctor, and I'm very aware of that.

sentences.zh: 我不是医生，我很清楚这一点。

blocks.html_en: One piece of knowledge I feel safe clinging to and saying out loud to the internet is that sleep is probably good for you. Everything seems to get better with sleep: your immune system, your cognitive function, recovery from physical activity, etc.

sentences.en: One piece of knowledge I feel safe clinging to and saying out loud to the internet is that sleep is probably good for you.
sentences.zh: 我觉得可以安全地坚持并在互联网上大声说出的一项知识是，睡眠可能对你有好处。

sentences.en: Everything seems to get better with sleep: your immune system, your cognitive function, recovery from physical activity, etc.
sentences.zh: 睡眠后一切似乎都会变得更好：你的免疫系统、你的认知功能、体力活动的恢复等等。

blocks.html_en: Unfortunately, I've always had a complicated relationship with sleep. Namely because my neuro is divergent.

sentences.en: Unfortunately, I've always had a complicated relationship with sleep.
sentences.zh: 不幸的是，我与睡眠的关系一直很复杂。

sentences.en: Namely because my neuro is divergent.
sentences.zh: 那是因为我的神经发散了。

blocks.html_en: Neurodivergent people tend to be night owls as a survival/recovery mechanism. Having some time to ourselves where no one is placing demands on our attention, behavior, etc, gives us some space to exist as our actual selves, not our "capitalism demands that I perform on this schedule, in this manner, to these people, otherwise I can't feed myself" selves. This means that our sleep routines can be either misaligned with our work schedules, or just irregular in general.

sentences.en: Neurodivergent people tend to be night owls as a survival/recovery mechanism.
sentences.zh: 作为一种生存/恢复机制，神经分歧的人往往是夜猫子。

sentences.en: Having some time to ourselves where no one is placing demands on our attention, behavior, etc, gives us some space to exist as our actual selves, not our "capitalism demands that I perform on this schedule, in this manner, to these people, otherwise I can't feed myself" selves.
sentences.zh: 拥有一些自己的时间，没有人对我们的注意力、行为等提出要求，这给了我们一些空间来作为我们真实的自我而存在，而不是我们的“资本主义要求我按照这个时间表，以这种方式，对这些人执行，否则我无法养活自己”的自我。

sentences.en: This means that our sleep routines can be either misaligned with our work schedules, or just irregular in general.
sentences.zh: 这意味着我们的睡眠习惯可能与我们的工作时间表不一致，或者总体上不规律。

blocks.html_en: If it's not clear yet, I have ADHD. This means that my brain never shuts up. Like, ever. This is not conducive to "winding down for bed." I developed a reading in bed routine from a pretty early age, which cuts down on distractions and acts as a signpost my nervous system can recognize as "ah, we typically fall asleep after doing this for a while, let's start that process."

sentences.en: If it's not clear yet, I have ADHD.
sentences.zh: 如果还不清楚的话，我患有多动症。

sentences.en: This means that my brain never shuts up.
sentences.zh: 这意味着我的大脑永远不会关闭。

sentences.en: Like, ever.
sentences.zh: 就像，曾经。

sentences.en: This is not conducive to "winding down for bed."
sentences.zh: 这不利于“放松睡觉”。

sentences.en: I developed a reading in bed routine from a pretty early age, which cuts down on distractions and acts as a signpost my nervous system can recognize as "ah, we typically fall asleep after doing this for a while, let's start that process."
sentences.zh: 我从很小的时候就养成了在床上读书的习惯，它可以减少干扰，并充当我的神经系统可以识别的路标：“啊，我们通常在这样做一段时间后就睡着了，让我们开始这个过程吧。”

blocks.html_en: Luckily I was diagnosed with ADHD pretty young, and I've been medicated since childhood. For people that are diagnosed as adults, I see one of two reactions to starting ADHD medication:

sentences.en: Luckily I was diagnosed with ADHD pretty young, and I've been medicated since childhood.
sentences.zh: 幸运的是，我很小的时候就被诊断出患有多动症，而且我从小就开始接受药物治疗。

sentences.en: For people that are diagnosed as adults, I see one of two reactions to starting ADHD medication:
sentences.zh: 对于被诊断为成年人的人，我看到开始多动症药物治疗时会出现以下两种反应之一：

blocks.html_en: • Holy shit, is this how normal people feel all the time? It's so much quieter!

sentences.en: Holy shit, is this how normal people feel all the time?
sentences.zh: 天哪，这就是正常人的感受吗？

sentences.en: It's so much quieter!
sentences.zh: 真是安静多了！

blocks.html_en: • Ah fuck, I'm autistic too.

sentences.en: Ah fuck, I'm autistic too.
sentences.zh: 哎呀，我也是自闭症患者。

blocks.html_en: If it's not clear yet, I'm autistic. The overlap between autism and ADHD is pretty significant, and in many cases the ADHD is just masking the autism. That's why some people go undiagnosed until they start medication. Not me though, I happen to be exceptionally good at burning mental energy in exchange for social skills. I was only diagnosed about two years ago, but there were signs.5

sentences.en: If it's not clear yet, I'm autistic.
sentences.zh: 如果还不清楚，我就是自闭症患者。

sentences.en: The overlap between autism and ADHD is pretty significant, and in many cases the ADHD is just masking the autism.
sentences.zh: 自闭症和多动症之间的重叠非常明显，而且在许多情况下，多动症只是掩盖了自闭症。

sentences.en: That's why some people go undiagnosed until they start medication.
sentences.zh: 这就是为什么有些人直到开始服药才被诊断出来。

sentences.en: Not me though, I happen to be exceptionally good at burning mental energy in exchange for social skills.
sentences.zh: 但我不一样，我恰好非常擅长燃烧精神能量来换取社交技能。

sentences.en: I was only diagnosed about two years ago, but there were signs.5
sentences.zh: 我大约两年前才被诊断出来，但已有迹象5。

blocks.html_en: Many people with autism have sensory issues, as in certain textures or sensations are abnormally bothersome. Imagine the reaction that some people have to nails on a chalkboard, or the sound of someone chewing, etc, but getting that reaction to a texture, certain kinds of touch, etc. Similarly, many people with autism have trouble regulating their body temperature to the same degree as neurotypical people. When it comes to sleep, this means that I have trouble feeling comfortable unless the temperature is just right, the weight of the comforter/duvet is high enough, etc.

sentences.en: Many people with autism have sensory issues, as in certain textures or sensations are abnormally bothersome.
sentences.zh: 许多自闭症患者都有感官问题，因为某些纹理或感觉异常令人烦恼。

sentences.en: Imagine the reaction that some people have to nails on a chalkboard, or the sound of someone chewing, etc, but getting that reaction to a texture, certain kinds of touch, etc. Similarly, many people with autism have trouble regulating their body temperature to the same degree as neurotypical people.
sentences.zh: 想象一下，有些人对黑板上的指甲的反应，或者某人咀嚼的声音等，但对纹理、某些类型的触摸等有这种反应。同样，许多自闭症患者无法像神经正常人那样将体温调节到同样的程度。

sentences.en: When it comes to sleep, this means that I have trouble feeling comfortable unless the temperature is just right, the weight of the comforter/duvet is high enough, etc.
sentences.zh: 当谈到睡眠时，这意味着我很难感觉舒服，除非温度合适、被子/羽绒被的重量足够高等等。

blocks.html_en: I also just moved to Portland (the cool one, not the lobster one). This is one of the rare cities in the US where air conditioning isn't a given. For most of the US this is like living in the stone age. I guess for a long time the climate was temperate enough that it wasn't needed, but the climate is changing has changed. It now gets certifiably hot as fuck in Portland.

sentences.zh: 波特兰现在确实变得热得要命。

blocks.html_en: My new apartment at least has a window unit, but it's in the living room, not the bedroom. There's also no ceiling fans. I also live in a high-rise, and heat rises. This means that my bedroom gets pretty stuffy at night.6 I bought temperature sensors to prove that my bedroom is the hottest room in the apartment and everything.

blocks.html_en: Temperatures are a little funky. The Fahrenheit temperatures seem to be integers whereas the Celsius temperatures are floats (Javascript "all numbers are floats" aside). The allowed Fahrenheit range is 55-115, and the Celsius range is 13.0-48.0. In control.set_temperature_* you can set the temperature to -1/-1.0 for MAX_COLD, or 999/999.0 for MAX_HEAT. In status.water_temperature_* presumably -1 is a sentinel value while the device is in standby. That's not documented, but seems reasonable.

blocks.html_en:
Aside: Fahrenheit vs. Celsius

To my dying breath I will fight Canadians and Europeans that Celsius is the better temperature unit for human-facing temperatures. Example: air temperature. 0F is really cold (0% hot) and 100F is really hot (100% hot). 0C is cold but not outrageous, but 100C is "100% dead." Also, as an AC connosieur, sometimes one degree F really does take you from "almost there" to "perfect." Throw off your chains. Be free of the coarse grained tyranny of Celsius.

blocks.html_en: I wrote bedctl in Zig, because that's my language of choice these days. I've used Rust professionally for about 5 years, and as a hobbyist since 2018. blocks.html_en: The CLI only has one dependency: an argument parser I extracted from the Tigerbeetle codebase, tweaked, and updated for compatibility with Zig 0.16.0. The library has no dependencies outside of the Zig standard library, using std.http.Client for requests. The CLI also doesn't link against libc, which is some magic made possible by Zig. Once the dynamic library is set up, it won't link against libc either. Also, since Zig natively supports cross-compilation, I can build for any supported system without needing access to it. Love it.

blocks.html_en: These are largely translations of the JSON objects, but we get to be fancy in a couple of cases. Rather than strings for control.display_temperature_unit we get an anonymous enum with .c and .f variants, same thing for control.thermal_control_status with .active and .standby variants. Anonymous structs and enums are one of my favorite syntax features in Zig. Being able to avoid naming something that's clear from context cuts down on visual noise and the mental overhead of coming up with a concise, unambiguous name.11

blocks.html_en: We also get a little bit of extra type-system assistance when setting temperatures in Fahrenheit. I forget the name of this particular feature, but when defining an enum you can give a semantic name to specific integer values, and let the rest just pass through as their literal integer values. This allows you to set both .max_cold and 78 as literal values, rather than needing to remember that -1 is the magic value for MAX_COLD. Unfortunately there's no way (that I'm aware of) to encode the additional restriction that the valid range of temperatures is 55-115F. For Celsius we're pretty much in YOLO mode because it's a float.

content_html_zh:
发表于 2026 年 7 月 18 日

目录：

  • Sleep
  • Chilipad 为什么选择这个设备 结构
  • API 关于 Postman 示例的说明 端点 设备控制
  • API客户端
  • 睡在上面

现在我引起了你们的注意，让我们稍微分解一下。

我最近购买了 Sleep.me Chilipad 2.0，该系统允许您使用薄薄的、充满水的床垫罩控制床的温度，该床垫罩连接到包含泵、储水器和恒温器的设备。 价格是 17001 美元。 是的，这很荒谬。 我知道。 我靠软件工程师赚钱，我单身，独自生活，而且没有孩子。 告我吧 我将立即提供一个严肃的、成熟的理由，但现在“啦啦啦听不到你的声音”。

这个想法是，如果你自己做起来有困难，卧室里没有空调等，它可以帮助你在睡觉时调节体温。该设备允许你整晚设置所需的温度，将其放在一周中某些日子的时间表上，等等。制造商还提供了一个开发者 API，所以立即就没有我不对此做一些废话的世界。

结果是一个名为 bedctl2 的工具。 我这样命名有两个原因：

  • 名称以 -ctl 结尾的“控制”程序有着悠久的历史。
  • 在我的脑海中，bedctl 发音为“bed cuttle”3，听起来像“bed cuddle”，很可爱。

Sleep

我们对人体如何运作的理解不断发展。 这很好。 然而，在社交媒体上的骗子、行业资助的有偏见的研究以及我前面提到的科学提供的不断发展的理解之间，你有时可以合理地说服自己，有证据支持“杂耍活鲑鱼可以治愈男性型秃发”的说法。 4

我拥有物理学博士学位。 我制造了可发出 80 个飞秒脉冲的激光器，并进行了量子力学模拟。 我对那次经历感到非常震惊，以至于我对科学相关（包括健康）头条新闻和社交媒体帖子的默认立场是“胡说八道，除非另有证明”，其中“已证明”包括但不限于“得到我的医生或医疗专业人士的共识证实”。 需要明确的是，这种不信任并不是对科学的不信任，而是对为了适应标题而剔除细微差别的不信任。 我不是医生，我很清楚这一点。

我觉得可以安全地坚持并在互联网上大声说出的一项知识是，睡眠可能对你有好处。 睡眠后一切似乎都会变得更好：你的免疫系统、你的认知功能、体力活动的恢复等等。

不幸的是，我与睡眠的关系一直很复杂。 那是因为我的神经发散了。

作为一种生存/恢复机制，神经分歧的人往往是夜猫子。 拥有一些自己的时间，没有人对我们的注意力、行为等提出要求，这给了我们一些空间来作为我们真实的自我而存在，而不是我们的“资本主义要求我按照这个时间表，以这种方式，对这些人执行，否则我无法养活自己”的自我。 这意味着我们的睡眠习惯可能与我们的工作时间表不一致，或者总体上不规律。

如果还不清楚的话，我患有多动症。 这意味着我的大脑永远不会关闭。 就像，曾经。 这不利于“放松睡觉”。 我从很小的时候就养成了在床上读书的习惯，它可以减少干扰，并充当我的神经系统可以识别的路标：“啊，我们通常在这样做一段时间后就睡着了，让我们开始这个过程吧。”

幸运的是，我很小的时候就被诊断出患有多动症，而且我从小就开始接受药物治疗。 对于被诊断为成年人的人，我看到开始多动症药物治疗时会出现以下两种反应之一：

  • 天哪，这就是正常人的感受吗？ 真是安静多了！
  • 哎呀，我也是自闭症患者。

如果还不清楚，我就是自闭症患者。 自闭症和多动症之间的重叠非常明显，而且在许多情况下，多动症只是掩盖了自闭症。 这就是为什么有些人直到开始服药才被诊断出来。 但我不一样，我恰好非常擅长燃烧精神能量来换取社交技能。 我大约两年前才被诊断出来，但已有迹象5。

许多自闭症患者都有感官问题，因为某些纹理或感觉异常令人烦恼。 想象一下，有些人对黑板上的指甲的反应，或者某人咀嚼的声音等，但对纹理、某些类型的触摸等有这种反应。同样，许多自闭症患者无法像神经正常人那样将体温调节到同样的程度。 当谈到睡眠时，这意味着我很难感觉舒服，除非温度合适、被子/羽绒被的重量足够高等等。

我也刚刚搬到波特兰（很酷的，不是龙虾的）。 这是美国少有的没有空调的城市之一。 对于大多数美国人来说，这就像生活在石器时代。 我猜想很长一段时间气候都足够温和，所以不需要它，但气候正在发生变化。 波特兰现在确实变得热得要命。

我的新公寓至少有一个窗户单元，但它在客厅，而不是卧室。 也没有吊扇。 我也住高楼，热气上升。 这意味着我的卧室在晚上会变得非常闷热。6 我买了温度传感器来证明我的卧室是公寓里最热的房间。

所以，回顾一下：

  • 大脑永远不会关闭
  • 我的血管里流淌着医用级兴奋剂
  • 我不喜欢闷热的卧室
  • 睡不好对你来说很糟糕
  • 请帮助我睡得更好

这就是为什么我说去他妈的，然后把内存钱花在了我床上的温度控制系统上。 剧透：这很好。

辣椒帕德

现在我们将讨论设备本身。

为什么选择这个设备

当然，在购买之前，我对“睡眠凉爽”景观做了很多研究。

我不想要像 BedJet 这样基于风扇的解决方案，因为我不希望它在夏天向我的脚吹冷风，即使在冬天向我的脚吹暖风听起来不错。

我认为最接近的竞争对手是“八睡”，但我很快就放弃了它。 最便宜的型号是 3000 美元，它需要订阅，内置了一些人工智能废话，等等。 PASS.7 说真的，为了继续使用你刚买的 3000 美元设备，别再订阅了。 不要购买该设备。

Chilipad 2.0，出于懒惰，我将其称为Chilipad，它的起价更便宜，因为它是模块化的。 您可以只在床的一侧购买一个单元以节省一些钱，这就是我所做的。 这也意味着如果您购买其中两个，您可以将它们设置为不同的温度。 我曾经住过的每一个伴侣在睡觉时都是一个人体熔炉，所以我可以看到这对夫妻有帮助。

该公司还做了很多其他非常礼貌的事情，为我达成了交易：

  • 即使已连接 WiFi，也有飞行模式。
  • 您可以使用附带的蓝牙遥控器控制设备，但我尚未检查或测试飞行模式是否是真正的飞行模式或只是“禁用 WiFi”。
  • 他们提供了开发者 API.8
  • 他们提供了一个 Postman“集合”来使用 API。
  • 他们提供在线 Swagger UI 和 OpenAPI 架构。

将设备置于飞行模式的能力告诉我，他们意识到这样一个事实：在你的床上内置一个联网设备可能会令人毛骨悚然，而且如果你的床加入了机器人僵尸网络，你可能需要一个故障保护装置。

建造

该装置由两个主要部分组成：

  • “底座”，即控制单元、恒温器、泵等。
  • 盖子，其中包含“水层”。

我会让网站上的照片来说明大部分内容。 码头位于我的床脚下方，靠近我卧室的门，因此热交换器不只是将热空气喷回我的房间。

盖子本身实际上是三层：

  • 底层
  • 水层（水循环通过的 TPU 膜）
  • 顶盖

顶盖拉链打开，这样您就可以将水层安装在您睡觉的床的一侧。 顶盖由触感凉爽的织物制成，并带有轻质衬垫，因此您不会感觉自己直接躺在水层上。

API

让我们谈谈 API，这样我们就可以讨论 API 客户端了。 看起来文档是通过拥有帐户来控制的，所以我将为您描述一下。 首先，您需要为自己生成一个令牌，就像大多数 API 一样。

关于 Postman 示例的说明

端点

API 非常简单，您有两个端点：

  • /devices GET：列出与令牌关联的设备
  • /devices/{:id} GET：获取有关设备的所有信息 PATCH：设置设备属性

PATCH 请求是您远程控制设备的方式。 我们稍后会讨论这个问题。

以下是对 /devices 端点的调用所得到的结果（我将编辑一些内容10）：

[
  {
    "id": "<redacted>",
    "name": "Dock",
    "attachments": []
  }
]

这是一个设备列表，其中每个对象都包含设备 ID、用户分配的名称以及附加到它的任何其他设备（我认为，不确定最后一个）。

以下是您从 /devices/{:id} 返回的内容：

{
  "about": {
    "firmware_version": "1.12.1358",
    "ip_address": "<redacted>",
    "lan_address": "192.168.8.69",
    "mac_address": "<redacted>",
    "model": "DP723NA",
    "serial_number": "<redacted>"
  },
  "control": {
    "brightness_level": 100,
    "display_temperature_unit": "f",
    "set_temperature_c": 21.5,
    "set_temperature_f": 71,
    "thermal_control_status": "standby",
    "time_zone": "America/Los_Angeles"
  },
  "status": {
    "is_connected": true,
    "is_water_low": false,
    "water_level": 100,
    "water_temperature_f": -1,
    "water_temperature_c": -1
  }
}

about 对象包含有关设备标识和定义的只读数据。 ip_address 是外部 IP，lan_address 是本地网络上的 IP。

控制对象描述了所有可控属性的当前设置。 这是特定于设备的，因此某些设备可能具有其中的子集，或者可能具有文档中未列出的键（例如，文档未列出 Brightness_level 键）。 Thermal_control_status 是待机或活动状态，仅表示设备是否正在主动控制温度。

状态对象包含有关设备状态的只读数据。 is_connected 表示设备是否可通过 API 访问。 water_level 是一个百分比 (0-100)。

温度有点奇怪。 华氏温度似乎是整数，而摄氏温度是浮点数（Javascript“所有数字都是浮点数”除外）。 允许的华氏温度范围为 55-115，摄氏温度范围为 13.0-48.0。 在 control.set_Temperature_* 中，您可以将 MAX_COLD 的温度设置为 -1/-1.0，或将 MAX_HEAT 的温度设置为 999/999.0。 在 status.water_temper_* 中，设备处于待机状态时，-1 可能是一个哨兵值。 这没有记录，但似乎是合理的。

旁白：华氏度与摄氏度 在我垂死之前，我将与加拿大人和欧洲人争论，摄氏度是人类面对温度的更好温度单位。 例如：气温。 0F 非常冷（0% 热），100F 非常热（100% 热）。 0C 很冷，但也不算太离谱，但 100C 就“100% 死了”。 此外，作为空调鉴赏家，有时华氏度确实可以让您从“几乎达到”到“完美”。 挣脱你的枷锁。 摆脱摄氏温度的粗粒度暴政。

设备控制

您可以通过发送仅包含要设置的键的控制对象来设置设备的属性。 例如，如果您只想设置水温，您可以发送

{
  "set_temperature_f": 73
}

文档中实际上没有详细说明这一点，但我从 Postman 集合中收集了它。 重要的是，您不需要包含所有键，也不需要将其包装在 { "control": ... } 中。

API客户端

我用 Zig 编写 bedctl，因为这是我现在选择的语言。 我专业使用 Rust 已有大约 5 年的时间，从 2018 年开始成为一名业余爱好者。 没有阴影，我只想要即时反馈和较低的心理负担。 zig build check --watch -fincremental 在我的 M1 Max Macbook Pro 上的更新时间为 75 毫秒，这意味着它甚至没有使用更快的自托管 x86_64 后端。 我所遇到的困难是学习新工具的困难，而不是“我如何阅读这个”或“我如何追踪这个特征方法的实现”。

bedctl 主要是一个库，而 CLI 是该库的一个非常薄的包装。 该项目当前的结构如下所示：

  • bedctl Zig 模块
  • 使用 Zig 模块的 bedctl CLI

我的最终目标是让 bedctl 作为高级语言的零依赖动态库使用。 我希望有人能够编写一个用于 Home Assistant 的 Python 包装器。 我不会这样做，但如果有人这样做我会使用它。 我还没有为动态库设置构建或界面，但这将在不久的将来的某个时候实现。 那时，我将为 CLI 提供一个单独的构建步骤，将其用作用于测试 C 接口的动态库。

此时项目结构将如下所示：

  • bedctl Zig 模块
  • 使用 Zig 模块的 bedctl CLI
  • libbedctl 动态库 为 Zig 模块提供 C 接口
  • 使用动态库的 bedctl-dl CLI

CLI 只有一个依赖项：我从 Tigerbeetle 代码库中提取、调整和更新的参数解析器，以与 Zig 0.16.0 兼容。 该库没有 Zig 标准库之外的依赖项，使用 std.http.Client 进行请求。 CLI 也不链接 libc，这是 Zig 实现的一些魔法。 一旦动态库被设置，它也不会链接到libc。 此外，由于 Zig 本身支持交叉编译，因此我可以为任何受支持的系统进行构建，而无需访问它。 爱它。

这些是该库运行的中央数据结构：

pub const Device = struct {
    /// The device's unique identifier.
    id: []const u8,
    /// The user-provided name for the device
    name: []const u8,
    /// The devices attached to this one, I think.
    attachments: []const []const u8,

    /// Read-only data about the identity and definition of the device.
    pub const About = struct {
        /// The current loaded firmware of the device.
        firmware_version: []const u8,
        /// The public IPv4 address of the WiFi the device is connected to.
        ip_address: []const u8,
        /// The LAN IP given to the device by the router.
        lan_address: []const u8,
        /// The MAC address of the control board inside the device.
        mac_address: []const u8,
        /// The model of the device.
        model: []const u8,
        /// The serial number of the device.
        serial_number: []const u8,
    };

    /// The current state of the device's controllable properties,
    /// indicating that you can request to change them.
    ///
    /// Availability may vary depending on device model.
    pub const Control = struct {
        /// The brightness level of the device's display
        /// as a percentage (range: 0-100).
        brightness_level: ?u8,
        /// How the temperature is shown on the device's display.
        display_temperature_unit: ?enum { c, f },
        /// The desired water temperature setting in Celsius.
        ///
        /// The allowed values are as follows:
        /// - MAX_COLD is -1.0
        /// - MAX_HEAT is 999.0
        /// - Normal temperature range is 13.0-48.0 inclusive
        ///
        /// The value will not be validated before sending the request.
        set_temperature_c: ?f32,
        /// The desired water temperature setting in Fahrenheit.
        ///
        /// The temperature may only be set between 55-115 inclusive.
        /// This will not be validated before sending the request.
        set_temperature_f: ?enum {
          max_cold = -1,
          max_heat = 999,
          _,
        },
        /// The state of the system's thermal control systems.
        ///
        /// Changing to "active" will turn the systems on, and
        /// a change to "standby" will turn them off.
        thermal_control_status: ?enum { active, standby },
        /// The time zone set on the device.
        ///
        /// May be set to any IANA time zone name.
        time_zone: ?[]const u8,
    };

    /// Read-only data about the status of the device.
    pub const Status = struct {
        /// Indicates if the device is actively connected and reachable.
        is_connected: bool,
        /// Indicates if the water level is low enough that it needs to be refilled.
        is_water_low: bool,
        /// Indicates the fill level of the resevoir as a percentage (range: 0-100).
        water_level: u8,
        /// Indicates the water temperature in degrees Fahrenheit (range: 55-115).
        ///
        /// If the device is in standby, this will be -1.
        water_temperature_f: i8,
        /// Indicates the water temperature in degrees Celsius (range: 13.0-48.0).
        ///
        /// If the device is in standby, this will be -1.
        water_temperature_c: f32,
    };
};

/// A device status response.
pub const DeviceStatus = struct {
    about: Device.About,
    control: Device.Control,
    status: Device.Status,
};

这些主要是 JSON 对象的翻译，但我们在一些情况下会很感兴趣。 我们得到的不是带有 .c 和 .f 变体的匿名枚举，而是带有 .c 和 .f 变体的 control.display_Temperature_unit 字符串，同样的还有带有 .active 和 .standby 变体的 control. Thermal_control_status 。 匿名结构和枚举是 Zig 中我最喜欢的语法功能之一。 能够避免命名一些从上下文中明确的名称，可以减少视觉噪音，也可以减少因想出一个简洁、明确的名称而产生的心理负担。 11

在以华氏温度设置温度时，我们还获得了一些额外的类型系统帮助。 我忘记了这个特定功能的名称，但是在定义枚举时，您可以为特定的整数值赋予语义名称，并让其余的作为其字面整数值传递。 这允许您将 .max_cold 和 78 设置为文字值，而不需要记住 -1 是 MAX_COLD 的神奇值。 不幸的是，（据我所知）没有办法对有效温度范围为 55-115F 的附加限制进行编码。 对于摄氏度，我们几乎处于 YOLO 模式，因为它是一个浮点数。

写这篇文章的经历是相当愉快的。 这是我第一次使用Io接口进行HTTP操作，所以一开始有点摸不着头脑。

我认为大多数人发送请求的心理模型是这样的：

  • 提供一个 URI
  • 提供字节或字符串形式的主体
  • send
  • 返回字节或字符串

由于 Zig 的级别较低，并且标准库中的 HTTP 客户端并不打算成为 HTTP 客户端的 Easy Button，因此情况看起来非常不同。 该过程如下所示：

  • 创建一个 URI 对象
  • 改变 URI 对象以设置路径
  • 使用基本 URI、标头和连接设置创建请求对象
  • （本例）发送请求头
  • 接收响应头
  • 这可以让你获得正文的阅读器
  • 从阅读器中消费以获得身体

确实感觉有点像对所有操作都使用手术刀级别的精度，但这没关系。 我确实喜欢使用 Rust，你可以在很多地方达到高水平，然后在你愿意的时候降到低水平，具体的汗水模式。 在 Zig 中，我感觉自己总是处于出汗模式。

将 GET 请求发送到 /devices 端点的方法如下所示：

/// List the devices registered to the provided token.
pub fn devices(self: *Self) Error![]const Device {
    var request = try self.requestGetPath(DEVICES);
    defer request.deinit();
    const response = try self.fetch(&request);
    const parsed = try std.json.parseFromSliceLeaky([]const Device, self.gpa, response, .{
        .allocate = .alloc_always,
    });
    return parsed;
}

/// Constructs a GET request configured with the authorization header.
///
/// Caller must deinit the request.
fn requestGetPath(self: *Self, path: []const u8) Error!Client.Request {
    var endpoint = self.uri;
    endpoint.path = .{ .percent_encoded = path };
    const request = try self.client.request(http.Method.GET, endpoint, .{
        .keep_alive = true,
        .headers = .{
            .authorization = .{ .override = self.token },
        },
    });
    return request;
}

/// Sends the request and reads the response. Caller is response for
/// freeing the returned slice.
fn fetch(self: *Self, request: *Client.Request) Error![]const u8 {
    try request.sendBodiless();
    var redirect_buffer: [1024]u8 = undefined;
    var response = try request.receiveHead(&redirect_buffer);
    var response_buffer: [4096]u8 = undefined;
    const response_body = try response.reader(&response_buffer).allocRemaining(self.gpa, .limited(64_000));
    return response_body;
}

忽略 parseFromSliceLeaky 中的 Leaky，我正在使用此处未显示的竞技场分配器做一些事情，这使得这不再是问题。 相信我，兄弟。

睡在上面

那么，睡在我价值 1700 美元的设备上是什么感觉？

过去几个晚上我睡得很好。 我戴着健身追踪器，所以我也可以定量地看到我的睡眠好多了。 我在“恢复性睡眠”阶段花费了更多的时间，并且整晚的“清醒事件”较少。 主观上，我也感觉休息得很好。 另一个数据点是，我睡在上面的一个晚上是在喝了一些威士忌之后，我从健身追踪器上了解到，威士忌通常会让我的睡眠变得更糟，但那天晚上我实际上睡得很好。 12

我只用了几天（我用了大约 3 个晚上的时间写了 bedctl），所以我们仍然处于蜜月阶段。

我第一次上床时，温度设置得太低了，因为凉爽的感觉，我几乎感觉自己躺在湿漉漉的地方。 调节温度很有帮助，我不再感觉自己躺在湿漉漉的地方了。 我还要说的是温度调整得很快。 我没有计时，但我认为它更接近 1 分钟而不是 10 分钟。 该设备只需不到 1 加仑的水即可充满，因此不需要大量的热质量来加热/冷却。

我仍然在拨动我喜欢的预定温度，但附带的遥控器（基本上只是一个带有触摸屏的大拨盘）使动态调整温度变得非常容易。 遥控器上的旋钮并不是最令人满意的触觉体验，但至少它会随着温度的升高而振动，因此您不必看它就知道要调节多少。 如果您必须戴睡眠面罩，因为您的卧室有华丽的落地窗，但窗户太高，无法合理安装遮光窗帘，并且您的卧室门上装有一个大磨砂玻璃窗，可以让客厅的光线进入，则触觉反馈有助于做出调整。 13

因为我的大脑不同，我仍然需要风扇的少量气流才能感到舒适。 以前我必须平衡这些事情：

  • 足够的气流保持房间凉爽
  • 将气流直接吹向我，因为大脑需要那种感觉
  • 没有太多的空气直接接触到我，以至于我的呼吸道变得干燥，让我整天咳嗽

现在我只是将风扇调到最低设置。

早上把温度设置得稍微温暖一点感觉很舒服，我真的很期待它在冬天预热我的床。 也就是说，我可以看出“我躺在上面的东西，床垫或其他东西”的透气性降低了。 需要明确的是，我感觉不像是躺在塑料或类似的东西上，只是与床接触的皮肤随着温度的升高而出汗了 1%。 还要记住，我的身体显然是一个弱者，无法很好地控制自己的温度，所以在同样的情况下你可能会幸福地干燥。

关于这一点，我要说的就是这些。 到目前为止积极的经验。 当我开始设置动态库时，我可能会发布更新。 夜夜。
